author: Edgerton
title: From Innovation to Use (article)
description: History of technology has been distorted by focus on innovation rather than technology-in-use. A genuine history must center old, geographically dispersed, maintenance-oriented technology.

author: Chandler
title: The Visible Hand
description: The 'visible hand' of managerial coordination replaced market mechanisms. New technologies created volumes and velocities that required hierarchies of salaried managers.

author: MacKenzie
title: An Engine Not a Camera
description: Financial models don't describe markets like a camera; they reshape them like an engine. Black-Scholes became 'performative' — adoption made the model more accurate, until 1987.

author: Hounshell
title: From the American System to Mass Production
description: Mass production (Ford) was fundamentally different from the 'American system' (armories). Ford's unchanging-product model reached a cul-de-sac by the mid-1920s.

author: Noble
title: America by Design
description: Technology and corporate capitalism are inseparable. Engineers designed both machinery and social apparatus (patents, education, management) to ensure progress reinforced capitalist relations.

author: Kranzberg
title: Kranzberg's Laws (article)
description: 'Technology is neither good nor bad; nor is it neutral.' Six laws synthesizing decades of scholarship: technology must be understood contextually, not as autonomous force.

author: Layton
title: The Revolt of the Engineers
description: Engineers developed an ideology of rational social management but couldn't escape dependence on corporate employers. The 'revolt' failed; reform gave way to accommodation.

author: O'Mara
title: Cities of Knowledge
description: Cold War science funding reshaped American cities. Research parks (Stanford, Philadelphia, Atlanta) were deliberate attempts to replicate Silicon Valley's military-academic-industry ecosystem.

author: Baker
title: Make Your Own Job
description: The entrepreneurial work ethic was deliberately constructed, not natural. From self-help to startup culture, Americans were taught to see precarity as opportunity.

author: Oldenziel
title: Making Technology Masculine
description: 'Technology' as a masculine domain was socially constructed 1870–1945. Engineers narrowed 'useful arts' to exclude women through language, boundary-making, and narrative.

author: Vinsel & Russell
title: The Innovation Delusion
description: Innovation-speak has systematically devalued maintenance, repair, and care. Our worship of the new blinds us to the mundane labor that sustains everyday life.

author: Neff
title: Venture Labor
description: Dot-com workers internalized economic risk as personal investment — 'venture labor.' Structural insecurity was repackaged as individual choice and opportunity.

author: von Hippel
title: The Sources of Innovation
description: Innovation often comes from users, not manufacturers. Functional source of innovation varies by industry; understanding this shifts how we think about R&D and intellectual property.

author: Oudshoorn & Pinch (eds.)
title: How Users Matter
description: Users are not passive recipients but active co-constructors of technology. Non-users and resisters are equally important shapers of sociotechnical change.

author: Wisnioski
title: Every American an Innovator
description: 'Innovation' was deliberately built into American institutions by traceable networks of experts. The innovator persona expanded from Cold War elite to universal aspiration.

author: Cronon
title: Nature's Metropolis
description: Chicago and its western hinterland were a single metropolitan system. Market relations simultaneously created an urban empire and transformed the landscapes sustaining it.

author: Agar
title: What Difference Did Computers Make? (article)
description: Computerization was continuation, not rupture. Computers entered where routinized computation already existed. The difference was quantitative (speed), not qualitative.

author: Mody
title: The Long Arm of Moore's Law
description: Moore's Law's demands for miniaturization co-evolved with transformations in how American science is organized — decline of corporate research, rise of university-industry partnerships.

author: Edwards
title: A Vast Machine
description: The opposition between 'models' and 'data' in climate science is false. Everything we know about global climate depends on model-data symbiosis.

author: Stevens
title: Life Out of Sequence
description: Computerization transformed what biologists do, not just how fast they do it. Biology adapted to the computer — data-driven statistical methods displaced hypothesis-driven bench work.

author: Küppers et al.
full_name: Bernd-Olaf Küppers; Johannes Lenhard & Terry Shinn
title: Computer Simulation (article)
description: Simulation is a fundamentally distinct mode of knowledge production — a 'third way' beyond theory and experiment — based on behavioral imitation through generative mechanisms.

author: November
title: Biomedical Computing
description: Computerization of medicine was not inevitable — it required deliberate campaigns to restructure the life sciences. Computing and biomedicine were mutually constitutive.

author: Leonelli
title: What Difference Does Quantity Make? (article)
description: Big Data in biology is not just 'more data' but changes what counts as evidence, what questions are askable, and how biological objects are constituted.

author: Leonelli & Ankeny
title: Re-thinking Organisms (article)
description: Databases are reshaping model organism biology — not just storing data but actively constituting biological objects and research communities.

author: MacKenzie
title: Mechanizing Proof
description: Proof is socially negotiated, not absolute. Different 'cultures of proving' emerge around computer verification. The machine is a 'social prosthesis,' not an oracle.

author: Küppers et al. (eds.)
full_name: Bernd-Olaf Küppers; Johannes Lenhard & Terry Shinn
title: Simulation: Pragmatic Construction of Reality
description: Simulations produce hyperrealistic virtual worlds whose relationship to reality remains fundamentally open. A 'generic instrument' spanning all disciplines.

author: Light
title: From Warfare to Welfare
description: Defense intellectuals transferred military techniques to city planning. Technologies failed but the technocratic power elite persisted.

author: Heilbroner
title: Do Machines Make History? (article)
description: A qualified yes: technology constrains social arrangements within a range, but does not determine specific institutional forms. A founding text of the determinism debate.

author: Bijker Hughes & Pinch (eds.)
full_name: Wiebe Bijker; Thomas P. Hughes & Trevor Pinch
title: Social Construction of Technological Systems
description: Three complementary frameworks — SCOT, LTS, and ANT — demonstrate that technological artifacts and social groups are mutually constitutive. The 'seamless web.'

author: Leo Marx
title: Technology: The Emergence of a Hazardous Concept (article)
description: 'Technology' is a surprisingly recent concept that fuses artifacts with vast sociotechnical systems. It mystifies actual human forces driving change — a 'hazardous concept.'

author: Winner
title: Upon Opening the Black Box (article)
description: Social constructivism opens the black box of technology and finds it empty — no normative commitments, no political analysis, just descriptions of how closure happens.

author: Ceruzzi
title: Moore's Law and Technological Determinism (article)
description: Moore's Law is a genuine case of technological determinism. The steady doubling of transistor density has been impervious to all social upheaval since 1965.

author: MacKenzie
title: Marx and the Machine (article)
description: Marx's account of machinery is more sophisticated than crude determinism — machines embody class relations and are designed to discipline labor, not just increase efficiency.

author: Otter
title: Socializing the Technosphere (article)
description: The 'technosphere' — Earth's entire layer of technological artifacts — must be analyzed as a social phenomenon, not just a material one.

author: Hughes
title: Networks of Power
description: Electric power systems evolved through phases (invention, transfer, growth, momentum). Systems in different places reflected different societies — 'cultural artifacts.'

author: John
title: Network Nation
description: American telecommunications was shaped by political economy — corporate strategy, regulatory politics, and competing visions of public vs. private networks.

author: Kern
title: The Culture of Time and Space
description: Technology and independent cultural developments jointly transformed how Europeans and Americans experienced time and space. Private time and democratic space emerged together.

author: Carey
title: Technology and Ideology: The Telegraph (article)
description: The telegraph separated communication from transportation. This rupture reorganized commerce, journalism, imperial administration, and the standardization of time.

author: Starosielski
title: Fixed Flow (article)
description: Undersea cables are not immaterial flows but fixed, vulnerable, geographically embedded infrastructure shaped by colonial routes and environmental constraints.

author: Hunt
title: Imperial Science
description: Submarine telegraph cables steered British physics toward field theory. Technology drove science, not the reverse — cable problems created electromagnetic theory.

author: Hochfelder
title: The Telegraph in America
description: The telegraph was revolutionary but its social impact was shaped by institutional, economic, and political contexts. Effects were differential by class, occupation, region.

author: Douglas
title: Inventing American Broadcasting
description: Radio broadcasting was socially constructed over 23 years. Inventors, corporations, the Navy, amateurs, and the press all competed to define what wireless meant.

author: Müller
title: Wiring the World
description: The global telegraph network was shaped by competing social visions of the 'Class of 1866' — commerce, civilization, peace — not just imperial strategy.

author: Stine & Tarr
full_name: Jeffrey K. Stine & Joel A. Tarr
title: At the Intersection of Histories (article)
description: Technology and environment have developed as separate fields despite sharing a fundamental object. Historians of technology must incorporate environmental dimensions.

author: Leo Marx
title: The Machine in the Garden
description: American culture shaped by collision between pastoral ideal and industrialization. The 'interrupted idyll' — the locomotive intruding on the garden — reveals a structural tension.

author: Leo Marx
title: The Idea of Nature in America (article)
description: Nature as separate from humanity has become intellectually incoherent. The first nature/second nature distinction offers the most promising alternative.

author: Kline
title: Consumers in the Country
description: Farm people actively resisted and modified 'urbanizing' technologies. Rural modernity was a contested creation, not passive reception of telephone, auto, radio, and electricity.

author: Parr
title: Sensing Changes
description: People know environments through sensing bodies, not concepts. When megaprojects destroy habitats, the crisis is corporeal — trained bodies become unreliable in the new world.

author: Steinberg
title: Nature Incorporated
description: Industrialization was an environmental event. Water was commodified and privatized for textile mills. Industrial capitalism is 'a system of ecological relations.'

author: Yergin
title: The Prize
description: Oil shaped modern capitalism, geopolitics, and everyday life. Three themes: corporate capitalism, national strategy, and the 'Hydrocarbon Society.'

author: Williams
title: Notes on the Underground
description: Underground worlds — real infrastructure and fiction — model what happens when humans live in manufactured environments. The psychological costs of enclosure.

author: Edgerton
title: The Shock of the Old
description: Technology-in-use tells a radically different story than technology-as-invention. The horse, condom, corrugated iron, and rickshaw matter as much as the airplane and internet.

author: Jones & Wiggins
title: How Data Happened
description: Data is not raw material but a historically constructed category. From Quetelet through eugenics through machine learning — each era's data practices encode political choices.

author: Bowker & Star
full_name: Geoffrey C. Bowker & Susan Leigh Star
title: Sorting Things Out
description: Classification systems are powerful invisible technologies. Every scheme 'valorizes some point of view and silences another.' The hidden architecture of bureaucratic power.

author: Bouk
title: How Our Days Became Numbered
description: Life insurance made Americans into 'statistical individuals' — lives transformed into commodified risks. Risk-making migrated from corporations to public health and Social Security.

author: MacKenzie
title: Inventing Accuracy
description: Missile accuracy is socially created, not technologically inevitable. Institutional structures and interservice rivalries sustained the drive toward precision.

author: Fourcade & Healy
title: The Ordinal Society
description: We live in an 'ordinal society' — continuous automated ranking of individuals through digital data. Classification situations shape life chances; outcomes look like fair consequences.

author: Porter
title: Trust in Numbers
description: Quantification is a 'technology of distance' adopted by weak institutions, not powerful ones. Objectivity gains authority where trust is weakest.

author: Bowker
title: How to Be Universal (article)
description: Cybernetics succeeded through rhetorical strategies — a new reading of history, universal language, 'legitimacy exchange' — not discovery of deep truths about nature.

author: Galison
title: The Ontology of the Enemy (article)
description: Cybernetics originated as a wartime antiaircraft predictor. The enemy pilot became model for the gunner, the nervous system, intentionality itself, and finally the universe.

author: Haraway
title: A Manifesto for Cyborgs (article)
description: The cyborg — machine/organism hybrid — rejects original unity and natural identity. Feminists must engage with technoscience, not retreat from it.

author: Erickson et al.
title: Cold War Rationality
description: The interdisciplinary campaign to replace deliberative reason with formal algorithmic rationality. From the Berlin Airlift to Kahneman & Tversky.

author: Medina
title: Cybernetic Revolutionaries
description: Chile's Cybersyn: political values live in organizational arrangements, not machines. Changing social relations around a technology changes what it does.

author: O'Mara
title: The Code
description: Silicon Valley was neither free-market miracle nor government creation — it was both. Massive but invisible federal spending plus entrepreneurial culture.

author: Lécuyer
title: Making Silicon Valley
description: Silicon Valley grew from three successive manufacturing communities — radio, microwave tubes, semiconductors. Military demand plus commercial diversification.

author: Wisnioski
title: Engineers for Change
description: A crisis over technology's meaning split engineering. The outcome was not reform but an 'ideology of technological change' — engineers as passengers on technology's wave.

author: Rankin
title: A People's History of Computing
description: Time-sharing networks enabled communal computing before PCs existed. The shift to personal computers was a loss — from computing citizenship to computing consumption.

author: Campbell-Kelly
title: From Airline Reservations to Sonic the Hedgehog
description: The software industry evolved through three sectors — contracting, corporate products, mass-market — each tied to new hardware generations and new business models.

author: Turner
title: From Counterculture to Cyberculture
description: Stewart Brand's network transferred Cold War techno-optimism into mainstream culture as countercultural liberation. Digital utopianism was constructed, not inherent.

author: Brunton
title: Spam
description: Spam is the 'negative shape' of online community. Wherever people gather on networks, spammers follow, forcing communities into self-definition and governance.

author: Aspray
title: John von Neumann and the Origins of Modern Computing
description: Von Neumann's impact extended far beyond stored-program architecture — encompassing scientific applications, automata theory, and legitimizing computing as science.

author: Abbate
title: Inventing the Internet
description: The Internet was continuously reinvented through competing agendas. Its design embedded Cold War military values — survivability, flexibility, decentralization.

author: Kelty
title: Two Bits
description: Free Software is a 'recursive public' — a collective maintaining the infrastructure through which it exists. Five practices constitute a novel form of democratic action.

author: Hafner & Lyon
title: Where Wizards Stay Up Late
description: The ARPANET was not built to survive nuclear war but to share computing resources. Licklider's vision plus informal collaborative culture drove the network's creation.

author: Mahoney
title: History of Computing in History of Technology (article)
description: Computing history must engage analytical questions from history of technology — systems, labor, professionalization — not just hardware origins and pioneers.

author: Light
title: When Computers Were Women (article)
description: ~200 women who were ENIAC's first programmers were systematically erased. Sex-typing classified sophisticated mathematical work as 'merely clerical.'

author: Penn
title: Inventing Intelligence (dissertation)
description: AI was a 'science of industry' from inception. The symbolic/neural-net dichotomy obscures that all founders borrowed from social science to reduce epistemology to code.

author: Jones
title: Reckoning with Matter
description: Failed calculating machines from Pascal to Babbage reveal the impossibility of separating mental design from material production. Failure generated our frameworks for invention.

author: Forsythe
title: Studying Those Who Study Us
description: AI researchers operate within an unexamined culture that 'deletes the social.' Expert systems fail because they encode narrow formalized knowledge, then blame users.

author: Dick
title: After Math (dissertation)
description: Translating mathematical proof into computer programs was not reproduction but transformation — 'reformalism.' New formal tools changed what proof was and what could be known.

author: Collins
title: Artificial Experts
description: Computers are 'social prostheses' whose apparent intelligence derives from human users compensating for deficiencies. Most human knowledge is irreducibly social.

author: Voskuhl
title: Androids in the Enlightenment
description: Eighteenth-century women automata were products of artisan culture replicating bourgeois body practices — not philosophical experiments or industrial forerunners.

author: Dreyfus
title: What Computers Can't Do
description: AI rests on unexamined rationalist assumptions. Human intelligence depends on embodied, situated capacities not capturable by formal symbolic operations.

author: Grier
title: When Computers Were Human
description: Organized human computation — groups performing calculations — was shaped by division of labor and mass production. These workers were displaced by the machines their labor justified.

author: Schaffer
title: Enlightened Automata (article)
description: Automata were demonstrations of political and economic order, not just philosophical toys. Machines performing labor served as models for disciplined workforces.

author: Galison
title: Image & Logic
description: Physics = semi-autonomous traditions (image, logic, theory) communicating through 'trading zones.' Against Kuhn's monolithic paradigms.
